docs.text: Catalog of pages by category. Updated on every ingest.

docs.title: Entities
docs.text: • entities/let-go — the Clojure-dialect bytecode VM in Go.

docs.title: Stack VM
docs.text:
The let-go compiler emits bytecode that runs on a stack-based virtual machine. Values are pushed and popped from an operand stack; instructions operate on the top of the stack. This is the execution substrate for let-go programs after compilation.

status: speculative — verify details against pkg/vm before promoting to stable.

docs.title: let-go
docs.text:
let-go is a Clojure dialect implemented as a bytecode compiler and stack-based virtual machine in Go. It produces a single ~12MB binary with ~8ms cold start, no JVM, and passes the jank-lang Clojure test suite. It supports AOT compilation to standalone binaries and self-contained WASM web pages, two-way Go interop, and most of Clojure (persistent data structures, lazy seqs, transducers, protocols, records, multimethods, core.async, BigInts).

See the stack VM for how compiled bytecode executes.

docs.title: apply
docs.text: apply takes a function and a sequence of arguments, unpacking the sequence as the final arguments to the function. Use it when you have arguments collected in a list or vector and need to pass them to a function as individual parameters.

docs.title: Signature
docs.text: (apply [f args] [f x args] [f x y args] [f x y z args] [f a b c d & args])

docs.title: defn
docs.text: defn defines a named function and binds it in the current namespace. Use it to create reusable functions that can be called by name throughout your program. It combines fn for function creation with binding to a name in the current scope.

docs.title: Signature
docs.text:
(defn name doc-string? [params*] body)
(defn name doc-string? ([params*] body)+)

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
(do (defn square [x] (* x x)) (square 5))
;; => 25

(do (defn add [x y] (+ x y)) (add 2 3))
;; => 5

(do (defn greet [name] (str "Hello, " name "!")) (greet "World"))
;; => "Hello, World!"

docs.title: fn
docs.text: fn creates an anonymous function or named function. Use it to define functions inline without assigning them to a name (useful for higher-order functions like apply), or define a named function for recursion. fn supports both single-arity and multi-arity definitions.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
((fn [x] (+ x 1)) 5)
;; => 6

((fn [x y] (* x y)) 3 4)
;; => 12

(let [adder (fn [x y] (+ x y))]
  (adder 10 20))
;; => 30

docs.title: let
docs.text: let creates a new lexical scope and binds symbols to values for use within that scope. Use it to define intermediate variables, break down complex expressions, or avoid repeating calculations.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
(let [x 5] (+ x 3))
;; => 8

(let [a 2 b 3] (* a b))
;; => 6

(let [x 10 y 20 sum (+ x y)] sum)
;; => 30

docs.title: when
docs.text: when is a conditional macro that evaluates its body only when the condition is truthy. Use it for single-branch conditionals where you want to execute side effects or return a value based on a condition, with nil as the implicit else case.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
(when true 42)
;; => 42

(when false 42)
;; => nil

(let [x 5]
  (when (> x 3)
    (str "x is greater than 3")))
;; => "x is greater than 3"
